title: All Saints' Sunday
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
### November 4, 2018 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

All Saints' Sunday falls on the first Sunday following November 1st (All Saints' Day), making November 4, 2018 the designated observance. The RCL readings would have been Revelation 7:9-17 (the great multitude in white robes), 1 John 3:1-3 (what we will be), and Matthew 5:1-12 (the Beatitudes), centering on the communion of saints and the blessed life. This falls in Ordinary Time, the longest liturgical season, positioned between Pentecost and Advent (which would begin the following Sunday).

---

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the midst of a contentious period following the 2018 General Convention (held in Austin, Texas in July), where progressive resolutions passed on marriage equality and LGBTQ+ inclusion, creating continued tensions with traditionalist dioceses. In Colorado specifically, the Diocese of Colorado under Bishop Robert Wright (elected 2011) had been navigating these denominational divisions while maintaining a relatively moderate stance. No major diocesan events were scheduled immediately around this date, making it a standard liturgical calendar Sunday for Denver's cathedral.

---

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver in early November 2018 was primarily focused on the midterm elections held four days later (November 6), with intense local campaigns for governor (Democrat Jared Polis vs. Republican Walker Stapleton), congressional seats, and state ballot measures. The Colorado weather was typical for early November—cool mornings with highs in the 50s-60s Fahrenheit, occasional snow possible at higher elevations. Capitol Hill's downtown cathedral would have been bustling with civic engagement and election-year intensity characteristic of Colorado's status as a competitive swing state.

---

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS

The week before November 4 was dominated by the final push before the midterm elections, with President Trump holding multiple campaign rallies and Democrats mobilizing get-out-the-vote efforts. The migrant caravan traveling through Central America toward the US-Mexico border was a major news story, with Trump framing it as an election-year crisis. The Pittsburgh synagogue shooting (October 27) was still in the immediate news cycle, with ongoing coverage of antisemitism and gun violence dominating national conversation less than two weeks prior.

---

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Brazilian presidential election had just concluded on October 28 with far-right candidate Jair Bolsonaro's victory, marking a significant rightward political shift in Latin America. The UK was navigating Theresa May's Brexit negotiations with the EU, with the government facing internal Conservative Party divisions and uncertainty about the final deal terms. Migration crises were intensifying across multiple regions, including the Central American caravan and ongoing Mediterranean Sea crossings to Europe.

---

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

America in early November 2018 was in peak election anxiety—hyperpartisan, polarized, and facing questions about democracy itself amid investigations into Russian interference and Trump's presidency. The #MeToo movement continued reshaping conversations about power and accountability across institutions (with further revelations emerging regularly). Culturally, the moment felt politically urgent and divided; a sermon on All Saints and the communion of saints would have resonated as a countercultural call to unity and transcendence amidst national fracture. The Denver Broncos were in a disappointing 3-6 season, providing modest local distraction from national turbulence.
